revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.
== Overview == ### Geography and Location The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
### Historical Development #### Ancient Sue Ware Kilns The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
#### Medieval Kosai Kilns After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
### Designated Cultural Properties - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 2011.
== Related Topics == * [[Sue ware]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]]
== External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]

Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>

revision.text:
{{Infobox historic site | name = 湖西窯跡群 | native_name = | native_language = | image =File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg | image_map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|34|41|37.8|N|137|32|03.5|E}} }} | caption =湖西窯の須恵器（[[愛知県陶磁美術館]]蔵・2018年8月12日） | type =[[窯跡]] | locmapin = | coordinates = | location = [[静岡県]][[湖西市]]吉美・笠子ほか | area = | built = | architect = | architecture = | governing_body = | owner = | designation1 = | designation1_offname = | designation1_date = | designation1_number = }}
'''湖西窯跡群'''（こさいようせきぐん/こさいかまあとぐん）は、[[静岡県]]西部、[[浜名湖]]西岸の[[湖西市]]域を中心に分布する、[[古墳時代]]後半から[[奈良時代]]にかけての[[須恵器]]の[[窯跡]]群、および[[中世]][[陶器]]の窯跡群である。[[7世紀]]から[[8世紀]]代に窯操業の最盛期を迎え、東日本を中心に須恵器の一大流通圏を形成した。
{{coord|34|41|37.8|N|137|32|03.5|E|region:JP-22|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka | lat_deg = 34 | lat_min = 41 | lat_sec = 37.8 | lon_deg = 137 | lon_min = 32 | lon_sec = 03.5 | label = 湖西窯跡群 | position = right | mark = Blue pog.svg | marksize = 10 | width = 250 | caption = {{Center|窯のある湖西丘陵の位置}} }}
[[File:Sue-stone-ware(flask type)-kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|湖西窯のフラスコ瓶（[[愛知県陶磁美術館]]蔵・2018年8月12日）|180px]]
== 概要 == === 地形と立地 === 湖西窯跡群は、湖西市域の南部に位置し、[[天伯原|天伯原台地]]の一角で、[[遠州灘]]に面した[[丘陵]]地帯（湖西丘陵）に主に分布する。分布域は、西に隣接する[[愛知県]][[豊橋市]]域や、旧[[新居町 (静岡県)|新居町]]域にも及び、その数は1000基を数えるとされる<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm 『モノづくりの原点・湖西窯跡群』（湖西市教育委員会パンプレット）]</ref>。湖西丘陵は、南側では[[崖|海食崖]]が発達し、遠州灘に沿うように急峻な斜面が続くが、北側は笠子川や坊瀬川、一ノ宮川、古見川などの浜名湖に注ぐ複数の小中河川が丘陵を著しく[[開析]]し、複雑な[[谷戸]]地形を形成している。これらの谷地斜面と湧水、産出される良質な[[粘土]]（湖西黄土・湖西土）<ref>[http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ 『湖西焼』湖西市商工会HP]</ref>を利用して、古墳時代後半（[[5世紀]]末）に[[窖窯]]による須恵器生産が開始された。
=== 歴史 === ====古代須恵器窯==== 湖西窯で初めて須恵器の焼成・生産が開始されたのは、5世紀末、湖西市吉美の明通り（あけどおり）窯跡とされている<ref>後藤2001 pp.118</ref><ref name="#1">鈴木2001 pp.142</ref>。続く[[6世紀]]代には、湖西市域以外にも、[[豊橋市]]や、[[静岡県]][[浜松市]]、[[磐田市]]、[[袋井市]]、[[掛川市]]などで地点的小規模な須恵器窯が出現し、それぞれに特徴のある須恵器を生産したが<ref name="#1"/>、6世紀末から7世紀に入るころから湖西での窯数が増え始め、静岡県内に流通する湖西産須恵器のシェアも増加し、7世紀から8世紀にかけて操業のピークを迎えた<ref name="#1"/>。各窯では蓋坏や碗のほか、高坏、はそう、横瓶、平瓶、さらに東海地域の須恵器窯（[[猿投窯]]など）に特徴的な丸底の「フラスコ瓶」などの長頸瓶や長頸壺を産出した<ref>後藤 2015 pp.6</ref>。これらの製品は静岡県内を始め、関東・東北地方の太平洋側の[[遺跡]]から出土しており、最北端は[[青森県]][[八戸市]]に達し、東日本に広く流通した<ref name="#2">湖西市教育委員会パンプレット</ref>。出土量の多い関東地方では[[古墳]]や[[横穴墓]]の[[編年]]研究に利用されている。また西日本でも[[奈良県]]や[[大阪府]]にまで伝播した<ref>高橋 2009 pp.75</ref>。古墳や横穴墓の副葬品として、[[関東地方]]では丸底のフラスコ瓶が卓越するのに対し、[[東北地方]]では高台を持つ長頸瓶が優位になる傾向が示されている<ref>高橋 2011 pp.51</ref>。これらの須恵器窯生産を主導したのは、神（ミワ）氏であったと考えられている<ref>後藤 2019 pp.84-85</ref>。
====中世湖西窯==== [[平安時代]]に入る[[9世紀]]には、他の[[東海地方]]の須恵器窯のように[[灰釉陶器]]の生産に移行することなく<ref>後藤 2015 pp.7</ref>、西隣の豊橋市[[二川窯(豊橋市) |二川窯]]の出現と入れ替わるように衰退するが<ref group="注">湖西市岡崎の小俣坂古窯跡でのみ灰釉陶器生産が確認されており、まったく生産しなかったわけではない。</ref><ref name="#3">鈴木 2013</ref>、[[12世紀]]頃に[[山茶碗]]などを生産する中世陶器窯として復活し<ref name="#2"/>、碗皿類、壺類のほか、[[京都府]][[仁和寺]]円堂院の[[瓦]]や、陶製[[五輪塔]]の生産などを行った。中世湖西窯は[[渥美半島]]の[[渥美窯]]（[[愛知県]][[田原市]]域を主体とする）と同時期に出現し、窯構造や生産器種などに多くの類似点を持つことから、共通の基盤のもとに成立したと考えられている<ref>安井 2013 pp.2</ref>。なお、渥美窯と中世湖西窯製品の[[胎土]]は、同じ[[天伯原|天伯原台地]]の[[粘土]]を使用しているため、両窯の峻別が極めて難しく、破片で出土することの多い遠隔消費地の[[遺跡]]では「[[渥美・湖西窯]]産」とされるか、一括して「渥美窯産」とされている場合が多いという<ref>安井 2013 pp.12</ref><ref name="#3"/><ref>佐藤・平原・三浦 2014 pp.96</ref>。
その後[[鎌倉時代]]の[[13世紀]]には日常雑器（山茶碗）主体の生産にシフトしつつ存続するが、[[14世紀]]初頭には廃絶した<ref name="#2"/>。
== 文化財 == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|久安2年（1146年）銘陶製五輪塔（[[愛知県陶磁美術館]]蔵・2018年8月12日）|250px]] * '''[[久安]]2年（[[1146年]]）銘「陶製五輪塔」'''： 愛知県[[瀬戸市]]の[[愛知県陶磁美術館]]所蔵の[[五輪塔]]型[[経筒|経容器]]。「遠海新所之立焼」の銘があり、平安時代末に湖西市新所の窯で焼かれたことがわかる資料であり、国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されている<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ 『陶製五輪塔 (国有形(工芸))』愛知県瀬戸市HP] </ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} ; 注釈 {{Reflist|group="注"}} ; 出典 {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 湖西市教育委員会・湖西窯跡研究会[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm 『モノづくりの原点・湖西窯跡群（湖西市教育委員会パンフレット）』]湖西市 * 鈴木敏則 2001「湖西窯古墳時代[[須恵器]]編年の再構築」『須恵器生産の出現から消滅-猿投窯・湖西窯編年の再構築-』第5分冊（補遺・論考編）東海土器研究会 pp.141～170 * 後藤健一 2001「湖西」『[[土師器]]と須恵器』[[雄山閣]] pp.118～120 * {{Cite journal|和書|author=高橋透 |title=東日本太平洋沿岸地域出土須恵器フラスコ瓶の編年--湖西産を中心に |journal=考古学集刊 |issn=18814476 |publisher=明治大学文学部考古学研究室 |year=2009 |month=may |issue=5 |pages=75-97 |naid=40016727364 |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R000000004-I10340733}} * {{Cite journal|和書|author=高橋透 |title=7世紀の東日本における湖西産須恵器瓶類の流通 |journal=駿台史學 |issn=05625955 |publisher=駿台史学会 |year=2011 |month=aug |issue=143 |pages=51-77 |naid=40018975960 |url=https://www.sundaishigakukai.com/駿台史学-総目次-著者一覧/総目次/第141号-第150号/}} * {{Cite journal|和書|author=安井俊則 |title=渥美窯の展開 (特集 第25回 日本福祉大学知多半島総合研究所歴史・民俗部研究集会 シンポジウム「中世渥美・常滑焼をおって」報告) |journal=知多半島の歴史と現在 |issn=0915-4833 |publisher=日本福祉大学知多半島総合研究所 |year=2013 |month=oct |issue=17 |pages=1-12 |naid=120005724539 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1274/00002553/}} * {{Cite journal|和書|author=鈴木敏則 |title=湖西窯における灰釉陶器と山茶碗生産 : 小俣坂古窯出土品から |journal=三河考古 |publisher=三河考古刊行会 |year=2013 |month=may |issue=23 |pages=73-104 |naid=40020524596 |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R000000004-I026588850}} * {{Cite journal|和書|author=佐藤由紀男, 平原英俊, 三浦一樹 |title=渥美湖西窯と常滑窯製品の蛍光X線分析 |journal=岩手大学「平泉文化研究センター年報」 |issn=2187-7904 |publisher=岩手大学平泉文化研究センター |year=2014 |volume=2 |pages=95-119 |naid=120005435456 |doi=10.15113/00008896 |url=https://doi.org/10.15113/00008896}} * 後藤健一 2015『遠江湖西窯跡群の研究』[[六一書房]] * 後藤健一 2019「(2)湖西窯跡群と猿投窯跡群の生産と流通」『日本考古学協会第85回総会研究発表要旨』pp.84-85 [[日本考古学協会]]
== 関連項目 == * [[須恵器]] * [[山茶碗]] * [[灰釉陶器]] * [[猿投窯]] * [[渥美窯]] * [[渥美・湖西窯]] * [[古墳時代]] * [[窯跡]] * [[湖西市]]
==外部リンク== * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ 『湖西焼』]（湖西市商工会公式HP） * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm 『KF：湖西窯跡研究会のページ』]（湖西窯跡研究会HP）

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: fixing date error
revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.
== Overview == ### Geography and Location The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
### Historical Development #### Ancient Sue Ware Kilns The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
#### Medieval Kosai Kilns After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
### Designated Cultural Properties - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 2011.
== Related Topics == * [[Sue ware]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]]
== External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kosai Kilns}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Japanese kilns]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Archaeological sites in Japan]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:History of Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Pottery]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kosei Kiln]][[Category:Kosei Kiln]][[Category:Kosei Kiln]][[Category:Kosei Kiln]][[Category:dd]][[Category:Kosei Kiln]][[Category:dd]] [[ja:%E6%B9%96%E8%A5%BF%E7%AA%AF%E8%B7%A1%E7%BE%A4]]

Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>

''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 2011.

These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
### Designated Cultural Properties - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>

revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.
== Overview == ### Geography and Location The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
### Historical Development #### Ancient Sue Ware Kilns The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
#### Medieval Kosai Kilns After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
### Designated Cultural Properties - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 2011.
== Related Topics == * [[Sue ware]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]]
== External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:kk]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.
== Overview == ### Geography and Location The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
### Historical Development #### Ancient Sue Ware Kilns The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
#### Medieval Kosai Kilns After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
### Designated Cultural Properties - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 2011.
== Related Topics == * [[Sue ware]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]]
== External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]

These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.

The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.
== Overview == ### Geography and Location The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
### Historical Development #### Ancient Sue Ware Kilns The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
#### Medieval Kosai Kilns After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
### Designated Cultural Properties - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 2011.

The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
### Historical Development #### Ancient Sue Ware Kilns The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
#### Medieval Kosai Kilns After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
### Designated Cultural Properties - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>

Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
#### Medieval Kosai Kilns After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
### Designated Cultural Properties - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>

revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.
== Overview == ### Geography and Location The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
### Historical Development #### Ancient Sue Ware Kilns The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
#### Medieval Kosai Kilns After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
### Designated Cultural Properties - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>

These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
### Historical Development #### Ancient Sue Ware Kilns The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
#### Medieval Kosai Kilns After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
### Designated Cultural Properties - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>

'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.
== Overview == ### Geography and Location The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
### Historical Development #### Ancient Sue Ware Kilns The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
#### Medieval Kosai Kilns After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
### Designated Cultural Properties - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>

'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.
== Overview ==
=== === Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== === Historical Development ===
==== ==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== ==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage ==
[[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== === Designated Cultural Properties === - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>

* Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015.
* Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 2011.

'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.
== Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 2011.
== Related Topics == * [[Sue ware]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:qq---qq]]" to ""
revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.
== Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 2011.
== See Also == * [[Sue ware]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]]
== External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:qq deprecated close]]" to ""
revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.
== Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 2011.
== See Also == * [[Sue ware]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]]
== External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]

By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.

Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>

revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.
== Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 2011.
== See Also == * [[Sue ware]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]]
== External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]

Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buqqhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with aqqitional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.
== Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buqqhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}

''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 2011.

revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with aqqitional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue ware]] across eastern Japan.
== Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production.
The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref>
[[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue ware flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. 118</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond.
Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref>
The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buqqhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials.
By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century.
== Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (1146), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === - '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, 1146)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/2011012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref>
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 2011.
== See Also == * [[Sue ware]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]]
== External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]

'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.
