revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue pottery]] across eastern Japan. == Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production. The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref> [[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue pottery flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. dd8</ref> During the [[6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond. Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd. == See Also == * [[Sue pottery]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]

Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue pottery]] across eastern Japan. == Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production. The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref> [[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue pottery flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. dd8</ref> During the [[:en:6th century|6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond. Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[Hachinohe]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the [[Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd. == See Also == * [[Sue pottery]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]
{{translated page|ja|湖西窯跡群|version=103528848|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[Sue pottery]] across eastern Japan. == Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[:en:Enshu-nada Sea|Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production. The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref> [[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue pottery flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. dd8</ref> During the [[:en:6th century|6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond. Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[:en:Hachinohe|Hachinohe]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the [[:en:Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd. == See Also == * [[Sue pottery]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]
{{translated page|ja|湖西窯跡群|version=103528848|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] across eastern Japan. == Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[:en:Enshu-nada Sea|Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[:en:Lake Hamana|Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production. The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref> [[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue pottery flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. dd8</ref> During the [[:en:6th century|6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond. Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[:en:Hachinohe|Hachinohe]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the [[:en:Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd. == See Also == * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]
{{translated page|ja|湖西窯跡群|version=103528848|comment=Imported full ja history}}

''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd. == See Also == * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]

=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the [[:en:Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] across eastern Japan. == Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[:en:Enshu-nada Sea|Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[:en:Lake Hamana|Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production. The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref> [[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue pottery flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. dd8</ref> During the [[:en:6th century|6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond. Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[:en:Hachinohe|Hachinohe]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the [[:en:Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd. == See Also == * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]
{{translated page|ja|湖西窯跡群|version=103528848|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q48754092]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] across eastern Japan. == Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[:en:Enshu-nada Sea|Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[:en:Lake Hamana|Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production. The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref> [[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue pottery flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. dd8</ref> During the [[:en:6th century|6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond. Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[:en:Hachinohe|Hachinohe]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the [[:en:Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd. == See Also == * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]
{{translated page|ja|湖西窯跡群|version=103528848|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[:en:Hachinohe|Hachinohe]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the [[:en:Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[:en:Lake Hamana|Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production. The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref> [[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue pottery flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]

''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd. == See Also == * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湖西窯跡群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q48754092]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] across eastern Japan. == Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[:en:Enshu-nada Sea|Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[:en:Lake Hamana|Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production. The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref> [[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue pottery flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. dd8</ref> During the [[:en:6th century|6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond. Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[:en:Hachinohe|Hachinohe]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the [[:en:Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd. == See Also == * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]
{{translated page|ja|湖西窯跡群|version=103528848|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湖西窯跡群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q48754092]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] across eastern Japan. == Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[:en:Enshu-nada Sea|Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[:en:Lake Hamana|Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production. The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref> [[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue pottery flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. dd8</ref> During the [[:en:6th century|6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond. Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[:en:Hachinohe|Hachinohe]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the [[:en:Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd. == See Also == * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]
{{translated page|ja|湖西窯跡群|version=103528848|comment=Imported full ja history}}

=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[:en:Enshu-nada Sea|Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[:en:Lake Hamana|Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production. The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref> [[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue pottery flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. dd8</ref> During the [[:en:6th century|6th century]], other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond. Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[:en:Hachinohe|Hachinohe]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the [[:en:Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd. == See Also == * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]

These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[:en:Hachinohe|Hachinohe]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the [[:en:Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum]], this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd. == See Also == * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湖西窯跡群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湖西窯跡群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湖西窯跡群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q48754092]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], with additional activity during the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] across eastern Japan. == Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[:en:Enshu-nada Sea|Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into [[:en:Lake Hamana|Lake Hamana]]. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production. The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref> [[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue pottery flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]

By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] and beyond. Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as [[:en:Hachinohe|Hachinohe]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>

Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]

Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd. == See Also == * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]

They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}, sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|en|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|WD=Q4696582|lt=Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum}}, this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湖西窯跡群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湖西窯跡群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湖西窯跡群]]
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, with additional activity during the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} across eastern Japan. == Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[:en:Enshu-nada Sea|Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into {{ill|Lake Hamana|en|Lake Hamana|WD=Q1150952|lt=Lake Hamana}}. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production. The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}, and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref> [[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue pottery flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. dd8</ref> During the {{ill|6th century|en|6th century|WD=Q8090|lt=6th century}}, other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} and beyond. Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region}}. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as {{ill|Hachinohe|en|Hachinohe|WD=Q191914|lt=Hachinohe}} in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}, evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}, sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|en|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|WD=Q4696582|lt=Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum}}, this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd. == See Also == * {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]

By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|en|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|WD=Q4696582|lt=Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|1=Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum}}, this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湖西窯跡群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湖西窯跡群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:湖西窯跡群]]
'''Kosai Kiln Site Group''' (湖西窯跡群, Kosai Yōsekigun) refers to a series of ancient kiln sites located primarily in modern-day [[Kosai City]], {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, Japan. These kilns date back to the late [[Kofun period]] and continued into the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, with additional activity during the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]] for the production of pottery. The Kosai kilns played a significant role in the production and distribution of {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} across eastern Japan. == Overview ==
=== Geography and Location === The Kosai kiln sites are situated in the southern region of Kosai City, on the Kosai Hills, which are part of the [[Tenpaku Plateau]] bordering the [[:en:Enshu-nada Sea|Enshu-nada Sea]]. The area is characterized by rugged coastal cliffs to the south and dissected river valleys to the north, formed by streams such as the Kasago, Bose, Ichinomiya, and Komi Rivers that flow into {{ill|Lake Hamana|en|Lake Hamana|WD=Q1150952|lt=Lake Hamana|1=Lake Hamana}}. These geographic features, along with abundant clay deposits (Kosai loess), provided the ideal environment for pottery production. The kiln distribution extends westward into [[Toyohashi City]], {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}, and nearby areas such as the former [[Arai Town]], encompassing approximately 1,000 individual kilns.<ref>[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Kosai City Education Board pamphlet]</ref> [[File:Sue-stone-ware Kosai kilns-in Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Sue pottery flask produced at Kosai kilns (Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum)]]
=== Historical Development ===
==== Ancient Sue Ware Kilns ==== The first kilns at Kosai, such as the Akedoori Kiln in Yoshimi, are dated to the late 5th century.<ref>Goto 2001, p. dd8</ref> During the {{ill|6th century|en|6th century|WD=Q8090|lt=6th century|1=6th century}}, other regional kilns in nearby cities also began production, each producing distinct styles. By the late 6th and early 7th centuries, Kosai's kilns saw significant growth, achieving a dominant market share in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} and beyond. Key products included bowls, jars, and specialized items such as tall pedestal dishes and long-necked jars, including the characteristic "flask bottles" associated with kilns in the {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region|1=Tōkai region}}. These artifacts have been excavated from archaeological sites as far north as {{ill|Hachinohe|en|Hachinohe|WD=Q191914|lt=Hachinohe|1=Hachinohe}} in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}}, evidencing a wide distribution network across eastern Japan. They also reached western Japan, including sites in [[Nara]] and [[Osaka Prefectures]].<ref>Takahashi 2009, p. 75</ref> The kiln production was likely controlled by the influential Miwa clan, who oversaw operations and trade networks.<ref>Goto 2019, p. 84</ref>
==== Medieval Kosai Kilns ==== After a period of decline in the 9th century, the Kosai kilns experienced a revival in the 12th century, producing [[sanban chawan]] (everyday bowls) and other items. Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}, sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|en|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|WD=Q4696582|lt=Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|1=Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum}}, this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd.
== See Also == * {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} * [[Atsumi kilns]] * [[Medieval pottery in Japan]] * [[Ancient Japanese kilns]] == External Links == * [http://kosai-city.net/kosaiyaki/ Kosai Pottery Association] * [https://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~takemori/takemori/kf001_.htm Kosai Kiln Research Society]
{{translated page|ja|湖西窯跡群|version=103528848|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Products included plates, jars, tiles, and Buddhist artifacts such as ceramic [[gorintō]] (five-element pagodas). These kilns were contemporaneous with and closely related to the [[Atsumi kilns]] in {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}, sharing similar production techniques and materials. By the 13th century, Kosai shifted to predominantly producing daily-use pottery but ceased operations by the early 14th century. == Cultural Heritage == [[File:Ware Kosai Shizuoka.jpg|thumb|Ceramic five-element pagoda inscribed with "Kyuan 2" (dd46), designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
=== Designated Cultural Properties === * '''Ceramic Five-Element Pagoda (Kyuan 2, dd46)''': Preserved at the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|en|Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|qid=Q4696582|lt=Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum|1=Aichi Prefectural Ceramic Museum}}, this pagoda is a [[National Important Cultural Property]]. Its inscription reveals it was crafted at a kiln in the Shinjō area of Kosai during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref>[http://www.city.seto.aichi.jp/docs/20dd012600025/ Seto City Cultural Properties]</ref> == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Kosai City Education Board. ''[http://www.city.kosai.shizuoka.jp/6346.htm Monozukuri no Genten: Kosai Yōsekigun (Pamphlet)]''. * Goto, Kenichi. ''Research on the Kosai Kilns''. Roppongi Shobō, 2015. * Takahashi, Toru. "Sue Ware Circulation in Eastern Japan during the 7th Century." ''Sundai Historical Studies'', 20dd.
